one_line_takeaway: By sampling diverse, high-likelihood harmful prompts from a diffusion LLM's inverted joint distribution q(x|y) rather than relying on a fixed adversarial-training dataset, DAT cuts worst-case (Best-of-All) jailbreak attack success rate from 77-93% (best prior defenses) to 18-36% on Llama3-8B and Qwen2.5-14B while maintaining comparable utility.
key_finding: On Llama3-8B, DAT achieves BoA ASR of 36% versus the next-best baseline MixAT-GCG at 77% (CAT 88%, LAT 94%, CB 94%, Default/undefended 100%). On Qwen2.5-14B, DAT achieves BoA ASR of 18% versus CAT 93% and MixAT-GCG 75% (Default 100%). The Inpainting attack (data-specific, diffusion-based) breaks all prior defenses -- ASRs from 77% (MixAT) to 94% (LAT) on Llama3-8B -- while DAT reduces Inpainting ASR to 32% on Llama3-8B and 14% on Qwen2.5-14B. A 'Diffusion-only' ablation (diffusion surrogate sampling without continuous adversarial optimization) achieves strong Inpainting robustness (23% ASR on Llama3-8B) but remains vulnerable to GCG (20%) and especially BoN (88%), showing both the outer-loop distributional coverage and inner-loop adversarial optimization are needed together. Scaling the number of unique diffusion samples per behavior (M) from 1 to 16 monotonically reduces Inpainting ASR on Llama3-8B from 54% to 22% while XSTest helpfulness stays roughly stable (~0.4), which the authors present as empirical support for Theorem 3.1. A likelihood-filtering ablation shows data specificity matters, not just data volume: training on the 16 highest-likelihood (StrongREJECT-filtered) diffusion samples per behavior yields 78% robustness (at matched compliance) versus only 39% for the 16 lowest-likelihood samples, and even a single high-likelihood sample per behavior (46% robustness) beats the full set of low-likelihood samples. On the utility side, DAT's XSTest score is 0.464 on Llama3-8B (comparable to CAT's 0.464) and 0.464 on Qwen2.5-14B (versus CAT's 0.544); the authors note CB's higher XSTest score (0.672) is likely inflated because CB's training explicitly incorporates the XSTest dataset. A Pareto-frontier analysis (Figure 4, varying lambda_KL and inner-loop attack-step count) shows DAT dominates CAT and MixAT-GCG across the robustness-compliance trade-off curve on Llama3-8B.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Distributional Adversarial Training (DAT) for LLM jailbreak robustness. It argues that existing adversarial training (AT) methods (e.g. CAT, LAT, Circuit Breakers, MixAT) minimize empirical robust risk over a fixed, finite training set and therefore address only the 'adversarial optimization error' (finding a bad perturbation within a local ball), while ignoring the 'data distribution approximation error' (the training set failing to cover the true distribution of natural harmful prompts). This leaves models vulnerable to naturally occurring, non-optimized prompt variants (e.g. past-tense rewrites, translations) that were never covered by the fixed dataset. DAT closes this gap by using a pretrained Diffusion LLM (LLaDA-8B-Base) as a generative surrogate for the joint prompt-response distribution: it fixes harmful target responses y from a dataset (HarmBench adversarial-training split) and uses diffusion inpainting (following Ludke et al. 2025) to sample diverse prompts x ~ p_diff_theta(x|y), i.e. it inverts q(x|y) rather than only modeling the autoregressive conditional q(y|x). Generated prompts are filtered by scoring their induced completions on the target model with the StrongREJECT judge, keeping the top-16 highest-likelihood prompts per behavior (1600 total). These diffusion-sampled prompts are then combined with continuous adversarial training (CAT-style embedding-space perturbations, an 'away' loss on the harmful target and a 'toward' loss on refusal) plus a KL-divergence retain-set regularizer against the pre-AT model, replacing CAT's cross-entropy retain loss and removing CAT's loss thresholds (changes applied to both DAT and the CAT baseline for fair comparison). The paper also proves a bound (Theorem 3.1): under a bounded robust loss and a total-variation 'conditional fidelity' assumption on the diffusion surrogate (E_{y~q~}[TV(q(.|y), p_diff_theta(.|y))] <= epsilon), the gap between the true population robust risk and the DAT surrogate risk is bounded by 2*M*epsilon.
threat_model: Safety-oriented jailbreak robustness for instruction-tuned LLMs, not classification-style input-invariance robustness. The adversary's goal is to elicit harmful content; the model must avoid emitting it 'regardless of which prompt happens to elicit it.' The paper explicitly distinguishes two vulnerability classes: (1) model-specific prompts, i.e. prompts with low likelihood under the true harmful-prompt marginal q~(x) but high likelihood of triggering a harmful completion for a specific model (most optimization attacks like GCG or BoN fall here, and such prompts transfer poorly across models); and (2) data-specific prompts, which have high likelihood under q~(x) itself and thus tend to transfer across models trained on overlapping data distributions (e.g. past-tense rephrasing, translation, and diffusion-model inpainting attacks). Attacks evaluated against the trained defenses: GCG (gradient-based suffix, 250 steps, search width 512, top-256 candidates), PAIR (20 steps, Gemma-3-12B-Instruct as attacker), Best-of-N (1000 perturbations, sigma=0.4, word scrambling/capitalization/ASCII perturbations), Direct Attack (1000 multinomial samples at temperature 1.0 on the unperturbed prompt), and diffusion Inpainting (1024 diffusion-generated attack prompts, from Ludke et al. 2025). A Best-of-All (BoA) ASR aggregates worst-case robustness across all attacks (behavior counted broken if any attack succeeds), analogous to MixAT's ALO-ASR metric.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitation: DAT currently relies on conditional sampling from a specific class of diffusion LLMs (LLaDA); they note unconditional sampling from generative models (as used for images in Wang et al. 2023) is an unexplored alternative. Additional limitations evident from the paper's own reporting, not asserted as limitations by the authors but visible in their tables: (1) DAT's own Inpainting ASR is not driven to zero -- 32% on Llama3-8B and 14% on Qwen2.5-14B remain, meaning the diffusion-based attack that DAT is specifically designed against still succeeds on a nontrivial fraction of behaviors; (2) BoA ASR for DAT (36% and 18%) is still far from the near-zero GCG/PAIR/BoN numbers achieved on individual attacks, i.e. worst-case robustness is dominated by Inpainting; (3) DAT's utility scores are not uniformly best -- CB has higher XSTest on Llama3-8B (though flagged by the authors as likely an artifact of training-set overlap), and on Qwen2.5-14B DAT's ARC-E (0.816) and ARC-C (0.584) are somewhat lower than the Default model (0.862/0.619) and MixAT-GCG (0.862/0.619); (4) the theoretical bound (Theorem 3.1) is conditional on an unverified fidelity assumption on the diffusion surrogate's TV distance to the true conditional, which is not directly measured/estimated in the paper -- only its practical consequence (more samples improve robustness) is tested empirically; (5) evaluation is restricted to two model families/sizes (Llama3-8B, Qwen2.5-14B) and 100 JailbreakBench behaviors.

datasets_used: HarmBench adversarial-training split (source of harmful behaviors/targets)
1600 diffusion-generated harmful prompts (top-16 per behavior, filtered by StrongREJECT score, generated via LLaDA-8B-Base inpainting)
quantitative_results: Llama3-8B BoA ASR: Default 1.00, CAT 0.88, LAT 0.94, CB 0.94, MixAT-GCG 0.77, Diffusion-only 0.88, DAT 0.36
Qwen2.5-14B BoA ASR: Default 1.00, CAT 0.93, MixAT-GCG 0.75, DAT 0.18
Llama3-8B Inpainting ASR: Default 0.98, CAT 0.88, LAT 0.94, CB 0.92, MixAT-GCG 0.77, Diffusion-only 0.23, DAT 0.32
Qwen2.5-14B Inpainting ASR: Default 0.99, CAT 0.92, MixAT-GCG 0.75, DAT 0.14
Llama3-8B GCG ASR: Default 0.39, CAT 0.04, LAT 0.09, CB 0.03, MixAT-GCG 0.04, Diffusion-only 0.20, DAT 0.08
Llama3-8B BoN ASR: Default 0.99, CAT 0.00, LAT 0.00, CB 0.56, MixAT-GCG 0.02, Diffusion-only 0.88, DAT 0.00
Llama3-8B XSTest utility: Default 0.660, CAT 0.464, LAT 0.444, CB 0.672, MixAT-GCG 0.456, Diffusion-only 0.536, DAT 0.464
Qwen2.5-14B XSTest utility: Default 0.748, CAT 0.544, MixAT-GCG 0.388, DAT 0.464
Inpainting ASR on Llama3-8B vs number of diffusion samples M per behavior: M=1 -> 54% ASR, M=16 -> 22% ASR (XSTest stable ~0.4 across M)

Likelihood-filtering ablation (matched compliance): 16 lowest-likelihood diffusion samples per behavior -> 39% robustness; 16 highest-likelihood samples -> 78% robustness; a single highest-likelihood sample -> 46% robustness

Sample diversity (mean pairwise SBERT cosine similarity, lower = more diverse): Inpainting 0.178, GCG 0.131 vs Inpainting cross-cell 0.280(?) -- as reported: Inpainting-Inpainting 0.178, GCG-GCG 0.131 (BoN cell 0.280), and cross terms 0.078/0.179/0.240 in the reported diversity matrix (Figure 3)

Theorem 3.1: |R_pop(theta) - R_diff(theta)| <= 2*M*epsilon, where M bounds the robust loss and epsilon bounds the expected TV distance between the true and diffusion-surrogate conditional distributions
baselines_compared: CAT - Continuous Adversarial Training (Xhonneux et al. 2024)
LAT - Latent Adversarial Training (Sheshadri et al. 2024), Llama3-8B only
CB - Circuit Breakers / short-circuiting (Zou et al. 2024b), Llama3-8B only
MixAT-GCG - hybrid discrete+continuous adversarial training (Dekany et al. 2025)
Diffusion-only - ablation using diffusion surrogate sampling without continuous adversarial optimization
